study_profile.label: TORQUE control-plane monorepo
study_profile.description: Node-based control plane with task execution, workflow orchestration, scheduled automation, and dashboard surfaces.
study_profile.reusable_strategy: Use the same artifact schema for other repositories, but swap in repo-specific subsystem and flow definitions as needed.

repo.name: torque-ai
repo.description: An automated software factory for Claude Code. Discover, plan, execute, verify, remediate, and release — autonomously across local and cloud LLM providers.

capabilities: Multi-provider task execution with verification and completion handling
Workflow DAG orchestration and dependency-driven task unblocking
Provider routing, fallback, and health-aware execution
Scheduled automation and background maintenance loops
MCP and HTTP control-plane surfaces for tools, tasks, and governance
Dashboard visibility for tasks, providers, workflows, budgets, and schedules

flows.label: Task lifecycle
flows.summary: Task requests enter through API or handlers, start in the task manager, route through execution modules, and finish in the completion pipeline.
questions_it_answers: How does a task move from submission to completion?

steps.label: Ingress
steps.description: Accept or shape task requests before execution.

steps.label: Execution start
steps.description: Instantiate task state, start processes, and coordinate runtime state.

steps.label: Routing and process control
steps.description: Choose providers, manage retries, and track process lifecycle.

steps.label: Finalize and publish
steps.description: Finalize task output, verification, and downstream side effects.

flows.label: Workflow lifecycle
flows.summary: Workflow handlers define DAG structure, the runtime unblocks nodes, and await logic surfaces progress and completion.
questions_it_answers: How are workflows created, advanced, and awaited?

steps.label: Runtime orchestration
steps.description: Advance workflow state and unblock dependent tasks.

steps.label: Progress and await
steps.description: Surface progress and wait for node completion.

flows.label: Scheduled automation
flows.summary: Schedules are stored in DB helpers, fired by the maintenance scheduler, and translated into tasks or tool executions by the schedule runner.
questions_it_answers: How do schedules and Run Now executions work?

steps.label: Scheduler tick
steps.description: Detect due schedules in the background loop.

steps.label: Dispatch
steps.description: Turn a due schedule into a tracked task, workflow run, or tool invocation.

flows.label: Tool dispatch
flows.summary: Tool definitions and schemas are registered centrally, then routed to handlers and post-tool hooks through the MCP surface.
questions_it_answers: Where do tool definitions, dispatch, and MCP transport live?

steps.label: Handler dispatch
steps.description: Route tool calls to handlers and normalize outputs.

steps.label: Transport
steps.description: Expose tools over MCP and server transports.

flows.label: Provider routing and retry
flows.summary: Provider scoring, routing, and fallback logic combine health, capabilities, and retries to decide where work runs.
questions_it_answers: How does TORQUE choose providers and retry failed work?

steps.label: Capability and health data
steps.description: Track provider health, scores, and capabilities.

steps.label: Routing
steps.description: Choose the best provider for a task.

steps.label: Retry and fallback
steps.description: Retry or downgrade tasks after failure or stall detection.

onramp.summary: Node-based control plane with task execution, workflow orchestration, scheduled automation, and dashboard surfaces. Start with the top entrypoints and canonical flows, then use invariants and playbooks to reason about changes safely.
first_questions: How does a task move from submission to completion?
How are workflows created, advanced, and awaited?
How do schedules and Run Now executions work?
Where do tool definitions, dispatch, and MCP transport live?
How does TORQUE choose providers and retry failed work?
first_invariants: Submission surfaces should delegate into handlers and the task manager instead of invoking providers directly.
Task completion should converge through the finalizer and completion pipeline so verification and follow-up hooks stay centralized.
Workflow structure, runtime unblocking, and await reporting should stay aligned on the same DAG semantics.

invariants.label: Task lifecycle
invariants.statement: Submission surfaces should delegate into handlers and the task manager instead of invoking providers directly.
invariants.why_it_matters: Task requests enter through API or handlers, start in the task manager, route through execution modules, and finish in the completion pipeline.

invariants.label: Task lifecycle
invariants.statement: Task completion should converge through the finalizer and completion pipeline so verification and follow-up hooks stay centralized.
invariants.why_it_matters: Task requests enter through API or handlers, start in the task manager, route through execution modules, and finish in the completion pipeline.

invariants.label: Workflow lifecycle
invariants.statement: Workflow structure, runtime unblocking, and await reporting should stay aligned on the same DAG semantics.
invariants.why_it_matters: Workflow handlers define DAG structure, the runtime unblocks nodes, and await logic surfaces progress and completion.

invariants.label: Workflow lifecycle
invariants.statement: Dependent nodes should unblock only from persisted workflow state, not from ad hoc in-memory assumptions.
invariants.why_it_matters: Workflow handlers define DAG structure, the runtime unblocks nodes, and await logic surfaces progress and completion.

invariants.label: Scheduled automation
invariants.statement: Schedules should create tracked task or tool executions; background automation should not fire invisibly.
invariants.why_it_matters: Schedules are stored in DB helpers, fired by the maintenance scheduler, and translated into tasks or tool executions by the schedule runner.

invariants.label: Scheduled automation
invariants.statement: Manual Run Now should reuse the same execution path as scheduled runs, with only the trigger context changed.
invariants.why_it_matters: Schedules are stored in DB helpers, fired by the maintenance scheduler, and translated into tasks or tool executions by the schedule runner.

invariants.label: Tool dispatch
invariants.statement: Tool schemas, dispatch registration, and transport exposure should stay in sync.
invariants.why_it_matters: Tool definitions and schemas are registered centrally, then routed to handlers and post-tool hooks through the MCP surface.

invariants.label: Tool dispatch
invariants.statement: Tool handlers should normalize output through the same MCP-facing contract regardless of caller.
invariants.why_it_matters: Tool definitions and schemas are registered centrally, then routed to handlers and post-tool hooks through the MCP surface.

invariants.label: Provider routing and retry
invariants.statement: Routing decisions should be derived from provider health, capabilities, and policy-aware scoring rather than one-off special cases.
invariants.why_it_matters: Provider scoring, routing, and fallback logic combine health, capabilities, and retries to decide where work runs.

invariants.label: Provider routing and retry
invariants.statement: Retry and fallback should preserve task ownership and emit observable state transitions.
invariants.why_it_matters: Provider scoring, routing, and fallback logic combine health, capabilities, and retries to decide where work runs.

invariants.label: Control-plane API
invariants.statement: Routes and control-plane transports should validate and normalize requests, then delegate into handlers or execution services.
invariants.why_it_matters: HTTP and transport surfaces that expose TORQUE task, provider, workflow, and governance operations.

invariants.label: Control-plane API
invariants.statement: Business rules should stay in handler or execution layers so API surfaces remain thin and consistent across transports.
invariants.why_it_matters: HTTP and transport surfaces that expose TORQUE task, provider, workflow, and governance operations.

failure_modes.label: Bypassed finalization
failure_modes.symptoms: Tasks appear done but verification, ledger updates, or downstream hooks are missing.
failure_modes.why_it_happens: Task requests enter through API or handlers, start in the task manager, route through execution modules, and finish in the completion pipeline.

failure_modes.label: Split execution ownership
failure_modes.symptoms: API or handler code starts doing provider work directly, creating duplicated retry and state transitions.
failure_modes.why_it_happens: Task requests enter through API or handlers, start in the task manager, route through execution modules, and finish in the completion pipeline.

failure_modes.label: DAG/runtime drift
failure_modes.symptoms: Nodes exist in definitions but never unblock, or await surfaces show states that do not match runtime behavior.
failure_modes.why_it_happens: Workflow handlers define DAG structure, the runtime unblocks nodes, and await logic surfaces progress and completion.

failure_modes.label: Silent schedule dispatch
failure_modes.symptoms: The scheduler fires but no task row, tool result, or completion record is created.
failure_modes.why_it_happens: Schedules are stored in DB helpers, fired by the maintenance scheduler, and translated into tasks or tool executions by the schedule runner.

failure_modes.label: Run Now path divergence
failure_modes.symptoms: Manual schedule execution behaves differently from cron, skips work, or bypasses persistence helpers.
failure_modes.why_it_happens: Schedules are stored in DB helpers, fired by the maintenance scheduler, and translated into tasks or tool executions by the schedule runner.

failure_modes.label: Schema/handler drift
failure_modes.symptoms: A tool appears in one surface but fails validation, lacks a handler, or returns an unexpected shape.
failure_modes.why_it_happens: Tool definitions and schemas are registered centrally, then routed to handlers and post-tool hooks through the MCP surface.

failure_modes.label: Opaque provider choice
failure_modes.symptoms: Tasks land on surprising providers with no clear health or capability rationale.
failure_modes.why_it_happens: Provider scoring, routing, and fallback logic combine health, capabilities, and retries to decide where work runs.

failure_modes.label: Fallback duplication
failure_modes.symptoms: Fallback creates duplicate work or loses the causal chain between the original task and retries.
failure_modes.why_it_happens: Provider scoring, routing, and fallback logic combine health, capabilities, and retries to decide where work runs.

failure_modes.label: Hotspot drift around server/tests/vitest-setup.js
failure_modes.symptoms: High fan-in (240) and fan-out (8) inside Validation and tests.
failure_modes.why_it_happens: High fan-in or fan-out files are common places for implicit coupling to accumulate over time.

failure_modes.label: Hotspot drift around server/logger.js
failure_modes.symptoms: High fan-in (180) and fan-out (3) inside Runtime core.
failure_modes.why_it_happens: High fan-in or fan-out files are common places for implicit coupling to accumulate over time.

canonical_traces.label: Task lifecycle
canonical_traces.question: How does a task move from submission to completion?
canonical_traces.summary: Task requests enter through API or handlers, start in the task manager, route through execution modules, and finish in the completion pipeline.

sequence.label: Ingress
sequence.description: Accept or shape task requests before execution.

sequence.label: Execution start
sequence.description: Instantiate task state, start processes, and coordinate runtime state.

sequence.label: Routing and process control
sequence.description: Choose providers, manage retries, and track process lifecycle.

sequence.label: Finalize and publish
sequence.description: Finalize task output, verification, and downstream side effects.

success_signals: Tasks move through a tracked lifecycle instead of jumping straight from request to side effects.
Verification, completion hooks, and persisted task output are attached at the end of the run.

canonical_traces.label: Workflow lifecycle
canonical_traces.question: How are workflows created, advanced, and awaited?
canonical_traces.summary: Workflow handlers define DAG structure, the runtime unblocks nodes, and await logic surfaces progress and completion.

sequence.label: Runtime orchestration
sequence.description: Advance workflow state and unblock dependent tasks.

sequence.label: Progress and await
sequence.description: Surface progress and wait for node completion.

success_signals: Workflow nodes unblock in dependency order and await surfaces reflect the same runtime state.
Node outputs and completion status are visible through the await and workflow handlers.

canonical_traces.label: Scheduled automation
canonical_traces.question: How do schedules and Run Now executions work?
canonical_traces.summary: Schedules are stored in DB helpers, fired by the maintenance scheduler, and translated into tasks or tool executions by the schedule runner.

sequence.label: Scheduler tick
sequence.description: Detect due schedules in the background loop.

sequence.label: Dispatch
sequence.description: Turn a due schedule into a tracked task, workflow run, or tool invocation.

success_signals: Due schedules create visible runs with persisted metadata and completion status.
Manual runs and cron ticks produce equivalent results for the same schedule payload.

canonical_traces.label: Tool dispatch
canonical_traces.question: Where do tool definitions, dispatch, and MCP transport live?
canonical_traces.summary: Tool definitions and schemas are registered centrally, then routed to handlers and post-tool hooks through the MCP surface.

sequence.label: Handler dispatch
sequence.description: Route tool calls to handlers and normalize outputs.

sequence.label: Transport
sequence.description: Expose tools over MCP and server transports.

success_signals: A tool defined in the catalog is discoverable and routed through the same handler path in MCP and HTTP surfaces.
Post-tool hooks see a consistent result envelope from handler output.

canonical_traces.label: Provider routing and retry
canonical_traces.question: How does TORQUE choose providers and retry failed work?
canonical_traces.summary: Provider scoring, routing, and fallback logic combine health, capabilities, and retries to decide where work runs.

sequence.label: Capability and health data
sequence.description: Track provider health, scores, and capabilities.

sequence.label: Routing
sequence.description: Choose the best provider for a task.

sequence.label: Retry and fallback
sequence.description: Retry or downgrade tasks after failure or stall detection.

success_signals: Provider selection can be explained by score, health, and capability data.
Retries and fallbacks reuse the same task state instead of creating hidden parallel paths.

test_matrix.label: Task lifecycle
test_matrix.rationale: Tests touch files that participate in this canonical flow.

test_matrix.label: Scheduled automation
test_matrix.rationale: Tests touch files that participate in this canonical flow.

test_matrix.label: Workflow lifecycle
test_matrix.rationale: Tests touch files that participate in this canonical flow.

test_matrix.label: Provider routing and retry
test_matrix.rationale: Tests touch files that participate in this canonical flow.

test_matrix.label: Tool dispatch
test_matrix.rationale: Tests touch files that participate in this canonical flow.

test_matrix.label: Control-plane API
test_matrix.rationale: Tests import or name-match files inside this subsystem.

change_playbooks.label: Editing Control-plane API
change_playbooks.when_to_use: HTTP and transport surfaces that expose TORQUE task, provider, workflow, and governance operations.
validation_commands: npx vitest run server/tests/api-server-core.test.js server/tests/v2-governance-handlers.test.js server/tests/claude-event-hooks.test.js server/tests/api-middleware.test.js

invariants_to_recheck.label: Control-plane API
invariants_to_recheck.statement: Routes and control-plane transports should validate and normalize requests, then delegate into handlers or execution services.

invariants_to_recheck.label: Control-plane API
invariants_to_recheck.statement: Business rules should stay in handler or execution layers so API surfaces remain thin and consistent across transports.

invariants_to_recheck.label: Scheduled automation
invariants_to_recheck.statement: Schedules should create tracked task or tool executions; background automation should not fire invisibly.

watchouts: Schema drift between routes, handler args, and tool surfaces.
Control-plane code bypassing shared execution or governance checks.

change_playbooks.label: Editing Task execution pipeline
change_playbooks.when_to_use: Task startup, provider routing, process lifecycle, retries, verification, and completion handling.
validation_commands: npx vitest run server/tests/bug-001-override-provider.test.js server/tests/fallback-retry.test.js server/tests/host-distribution.test.js server/tests/process-lifecycle.test.js

invariants_to_recheck.label: Provider routing and retry
invariants_to_recheck.statement: Routing decisions should be derived from provider health, capabilities, and policy-aware scoring rather than one-off special cases.

invariants_to_recheck.label: Provider routing and retry
invariants_to_recheck.statement: Retry and fallback should preserve task ownership and emit observable state transitions.

invariants_to_recheck.label: Task lifecycle
invariants_to_recheck.statement: Submission surfaces should delegate into handlers and the task manager instead of invoking providers directly.

watchouts: Retries or completion logic that fork the lifecycle in more than one place.
Provider-specific execution leaking out of the routing and adapter layers.

change_playbooks.label: Editing Workflow orchestration
change_playbooks.when_to_use: DAG workflow creation, await logic, diffusion planning, and workflow runtime coordination.

invariants_to_recheck.label: Workflow lifecycle
invariants_to_recheck.statement: Workflow structure, runtime unblocking, and await reporting should stay aligned on the same DAG semantics.

invariants_to_recheck.label: Workflow lifecycle
invariants_to_recheck.statement: Dependent nodes should unblock only from persisted workflow state, not from ad hoc in-memory assumptions.

watchouts: New workflow nodes that do not participate in await/progress surfaces.
Dependency edges that are implied in code but not encoded in the DAG.

change_playbooks.label: Editing Persistence and scheduling
change_playbooks.when_to_use: SQLite-backed state, queues, schedules, provider stats, and workflow/task metadata.
validation_commands: npx vitest run server/tests/api-server.test.js server/tests/test-container-helper.js server/tests/v2-health-models.test.js server/tests/handler-task-core-extended.test.js
watchouts: Bypassing helper APIs when creating schedule or task rows.
Losing correspondence between scheduled actions and tracked runtime state.

change_playbooks.label: Editing Provider adapters
change_playbooks.when_to_use: Provider registry, CLI/API adapters, prompts, and provider-specific execution logic.
watchouts: One-off provider logic baked into routing or handler layers.
Health or capability signals that no longer reflect adapter behavior.

change_playbooks.label: Editing Tool and MCP surface
change_playbooks.when_to_use: Tool catalog, schemas, dispatch, protocol transport, and MCP-facing integration points.
validation_commands: npx vitest run server/tests/mcp-sse.test.js server/tests/mcp-platform.test.js server/tests/mcp-streamable-http.test.js server/tests/tools-aggregator.test.js

invariants_to_recheck.label: Tool dispatch
invariants_to_recheck.statement: Tool schemas, dispatch registration, and transport exposure should stay in sync.

invariants_to_recheck.label: Tool dispatch
invariants_to_recheck.statement: Tool handlers should normalize output through the same MCP-facing contract regardless of caller.

invariants_to_recheck.label: Scheduled automation
invariants_to_recheck.statement: Schedules should create tracked task or tool executions; background automation should not fire invisibly.

watchouts: New tools added to schemas but not wired into handlers.
Post-tool hooks depending on inconsistent response shapes.

change_playbooks.label: Editing Runtime core
change_playbooks.when_to_use: Root runtime modules that bootstrap configuration, logging, containers, and shared services.
validation_commands: npx vitest run server/tests/test-container-helper.js server/tests/api-middleware.test.js server/tests/baseline-all-models.js server/tests/baseline-runner.js
watchouts: Feature-specific behavior leaking into root bootstrap files.
Cross-cutting changes that silently alter the assumptions of many downstream modules.

change_playbooks.label: Editing Dashboard UI
change_playbooks.when_to_use: Interactive UI code, client bootstraps, and presentation-layer state.
validation_commands: cd dashboard && npm run test -- --run src/App.test.jsx src/components/TaskDetailDrawer.test.jsx src/components/TaskSubmitForm.test.jsx src/components/LoadingSkeleton.test.jsx
watchouts: UI-only state diverging from the server model.
Controls that fire bespoke endpoints instead of the shared API surface.

change_playbooks.label: Tracing Task lifecycle
change_playbooks.when_to_use: Task requests enter through API or handlers, start in the task manager, route through execution modules, and finish in the completion pipeline.
validation_commands: npx vitest run server/tests/codebase-study.test.js server/tests/completion-pipeline.test.js server/tests/fallback-retry.test.js server/tests/host-distribution.test.js

invariants_to_recheck.label: Task lifecycle
invariants_to_recheck.statement: Submission surfaces should delegate into handlers and the task manager instead of invoking providers directly.

invariants_to_recheck.label: Task lifecycle
invariants_to_recheck.statement: Task completion should converge through the finalizer and completion pipeline so verification and follow-up hooks stay centralized.

invariants_to_recheck.label: Provider routing and retry
invariants_to_recheck.statement: Routing decisions should be derived from provider health, capabilities, and policy-aware scoring rather than one-off special cases.
